## ANALYTIC-DPM: AN ANALYTIC ESTIMATE OF THE - OPTIMAL REVERSE VARIANCE IN DIFFUSION PROB ABILISTIC MODELS

## **Fan Bao**[1] _[∗]_ **, Chongxuan Li**[2][3] _[†]_ **, Jun Zhu**[1] _[†]_ **, Bo Zhang**[1]

> 1Dept. of Comp. Sci. & Tech., Institute for AI, Tsinghua-Huawei Joint Center for AI BNRist Center, State Key Lab for Intell. Tech. & Sys., Tsinghua University, Beijing, China 2Gaoling School of Artificial Intelligence, Renmin University of China, Beijing, China 3Beijing Key Laboratory of Big Data Management and Analysis Methods , Beijing, China bf19@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn,chongxuanli1991@gmail.com, _{_ dcszj, dcszb _}_ @tsinghua.edu.cn

## ABSTRACT

Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) represent a class of powerful generative models. Despite their success, the inference of DPMs is expensive since it generally needs to iterate over thousands of timesteps. A key problem in the inference is to estimate the variance in each timestep of the reverse process. In this work, we present a surprising result that both the optimal reverse variance and the corresponding optimal KL divergence of a DPM have analytic forms w.r.t. its score function. Building upon it, we propose _Analytic-DPM_ , a training-free inference framework that estimates the analytic forms of the variance and KL divergence using the Monte Carlo method and a pretrained score-based model. Further, to correct the potential bias caused by the score-based model, we derive both lower and upper bounds of the optimal variance and clip the estimate for a better result. Empirically, our analytic-DPM improves the log-likelihood of various DPMs, produces high-quality samples, and meanwhile enjoys a 20 _×_ to 80 _×_ speed up.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A diffusion process gradually adds noise to a data distribution over a series of timesteps. By learning to reverse it, diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020b) define a data generative process. Recently, it is shown that DPMs are able to produce high-quality samples (Ho et al., 2020; Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021; Song et al., 2020b; Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021), which are comparable or even superior to the current state-of-the-art GAN models (Goodfellow et al., 2014; Brock et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Karras et al., 2020b).

Despite their success, the inference of DPMs (e.g., sampling and density evaluation) often requires to iterate over thousands of timesteps, which is two or three orders of magnitude slower (Song et al., 2020a) than other generative models such as GANs. A key problem in the inference is to estimate the variance in each timestep of the reverse process. Most of the prior works use a handcrafted value for all timesteps, which usually run a long chain to obtain a reasonable sample and density value (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021). Nichol & Dhariwal (2021) attempt to improve the efficiency of sampling by learning a variance network in the reverse process. However, it still needs a relatively long trajectory to get a reasonable log-likelihood (see Appendix E in Nichol & Dhariwal (2021)).

In this work, we present a surprising result that both the optimal reverse variance and the corresponding optimal KL divergence of a DPM have analytic forms w.r.t. its score function (i.e., the gradient of a log density). Building upon it, we propose _Analytic-DPM_ , a training-free inference framework to improve the efficiency of a pretrained DPM while achieving comparable or even superior performance. Analytic-DPM estimates the analytic forms of the variance and KL divergence using the Monte Carlo method and the score-based model in the pretrained DPM. The corresponding trajectory is calculated via a dynamic programming algorithm (Watson et al., 2021). Further, to

correct the potential bias caused by the score-based model, we derive both lower and upper bounds of the optimal variance and clip its estimate for a better result. Finally, we reveal an interesting relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix.

Analytic-DPM is applicable to a variety of DPMs (Ho et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020a; Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021) in a plug-and-play manner. Empirically, Analytic-DPM consistently improves the log-likelihood of these DPMs and meanwhile enjoys a 20 _×_ to 40 _×_ speed up. Besides, AnalyticDPM also consistently improves the sample quality of DDIMs (Song et al., 2020a) and requires up to 50 timesteps (which is a 20 _×_ to 80 _×_ speed up compared to the full timesteps) to achieve a comparable FID to the corresponding baseline.

The proof of Theorem 1 consists of three key steps:

- The first step (see Lemma 9) is known as the _moment matching_ (Minka, 2013), which states that approximating arbitrary density by a Gaussian density under the KL divergence is equivalent to setting the first two moments of the two densities as the same. To our knowledge, the connection of moment matching and DPMs has not been revealed before.

- In the third step (see Lemma 11), we surprisingly find that the second moment of _q_ ( _**x**_ 0 _|_ _**x** n_ ) can be represented by the score function, and we plug the score representation into the second moment of _q_ ( _**x** n−_ 1 _|_ _**x** n_ ) to get the final results in Theorem 1.

## 5 RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE SCORE FUNCTION AND THE DATA COVARIANCE MATRIX

In this part, we further reveal a relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix. Indeed, the data covariance matrix can be decomposed to the sum of E _q_ ( _**x** n_ )Cov _q_ ( _**x**_ 0 _|_ _**x** n_ )[ _**x**_ 0] and Cov _q_ ( _**x** n_ )E _q_ ( _**x**_ 0 _|_ _**x** n_ )[ _**x**_ 0], where the first term can be represented by the score function. Further, the second term is negligible when _n_ is sufficiently large because _**x**_ 0 and _**x** n_ are almost independent. In such cases, the data covariance matrix is almost determined by the score function. Currently, the relationship is purely theoretical and its practical implication is unclear. See details in Appendix A.5.

## 6 EXPERIMENTS

We consider the DDPM forward process ( _λ_[2] _n_[=] _[β]_[˜] _[n]_[)][and][the][DDIM][forward][process][(] _[λ]_[2] _n_[=][0][),] which are the two most commonly used special cases of Eq. (2). We denote our method, which uses the analytic estimate _σn_[2][=] _[σ]_[ˆ] _n_[2][,][as] _[Analytic-DPM]_[,][and][explicitly][call][it] _[Analytic-DDPM]_[or] _Analytic-DDIM_ according to which forward process is used. We compare our Analytic-DPM with the original DDPM (Ho et al., 2020), where the reverse variance is either _σn_[2][=] _[β]_[˜] _[n]_[or] _[ σ] n_[2][=] _[β][n]_[, as] well as the original DDIM (Song et al., 2020a), where the reverse variance is _σn_[2][=] _[ λ]_[2] _n_[= 0][.]

We adopt two methods to get the trajectory for both the analytic-DPM and baselines. The first one is even trajectory (ET) (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021), where the timesteps are determined according to a fixed stride (see details in Appendix F.4). The second one is optimal trajectory (OT) (Watson et al., 2021), where the timesteps are calculated via dynamic programming (see Section 4). Note that the baselines calculate the OT based on the _L_ vb with their handcrafted variances (Watson et al., 2021).

We apply our Analytic-DPM to three pretrained score-based models provided by prior works (Ho et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020a; Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021), as well as two score-based models trained by ourselves. The pretrained score-based models are trained on CelebA 64x64 (Liu et al., 2015), ImageNet 64x64 (Deng et al., 2009) and LSUN Bedroom (Yu et al., 2015) respectively. Our score-based models are trained on CIFAR10 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009) with two different forward noise schedules: the linear schedule (LS) (Ho et al., 2020) and the cosine schedule (CS) (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021). We denote them as CIFAR10 (LS) and CIFAR10 (CS) respectively. The number of the full timesteps _N_ is 4000 for ImageNet 64x64 and 1000 for other datasets. During sampling, we only display the mean of _p_ ( _**x**_ 0 _|_ _**x**_ 1) and discard the noise following Ho et al. (2020), and we additionally clip the noise scale _σ_ 2 of _p_ ( _**x**_ 1 _|_ _**x**_ 2) for all methods compared in Table 2 (see details in Appendix F.2 and its ablation study in Appendix G.4). See more experimental details in Appendix F.

## 6.1 LIKELIHOOD RESULTS

Since _λ_[2] _n_[=][0][in][the][DDIM][forward][process,][its][variational][bound] _[L]_[vb][is][infinite.][Thereby,][we] only consider the likelihood results under the DDPM forward process. As shown in Table 1, on all three datasets, our Analytic-DPM consistently improves the likelihood results of the original DDPM using both ET and OT. Remarkably, using a much shorter trajectory (i.e., a much less inference time), Analytic-DPM with OT can still outperform the baselines. In Table 1, we select the minimum _K_ such that analytic-DPM can outperform the baselines with full timesteps and underline the corresponding results. Specifically, analytic-DPM enjoys a 40 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (LS) and ImageNet 64x64, and a 20 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (CS) and CelebA 64x64.

Although we mainly focus on learning-free strategies of choosing the reverse variance, we also compare to another strong baseline that predicts the variance by a neural network (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021). With full timesteps, Analytic-DPM achieves a NLL of 3.61 on ImageNet 64x64, which is very close to 3.57 reported in Nichol & Dhariwal (2021). Besides, while Nichol & Dhariwal (2021) report that the ET drastically reduces the log-likelihood performance of their neural-networkparameterized variance, Analytic-DPM performs well with the ET. See details in Appendix G.6.

## 6.2 SAMPLE QUALITY

As for the sample quality, we consider the commonly used FID score (Heusel et al., 2017), where a lower value indicates a better sample quality. As shown in Table 2, under trajectories of different _K_ , our Analytic-DDIM consistently improves the sample quality of the original DDIM. This allows us to generate high-quality samples with less than 50 timesteps, which results in a 20 _×_ to 80 _×_ speed up compared to the full timesteps. Indeed, in most cases, Analytic-DDIM only requires up to 50 timesteps to get a similar performance to the baselines. Besides, Analytic-DDPM also improves the sample quality of the original DDPM in most cases. For fairness, we use the ET implementation in Nichol & Dhariwal (2021) for all results in Table 2. We also report the results on CelebA 64x64 using a slightly different implementation of the ET following Song et al. (2020a) in Appendix G.7, and our Analytic-DPM is still effective. We show generated samples in Appendix G.9.

We observe that Analytic-DDPM does not always outperform the baseline under the FID metric, which is inconsistent with the likelihood results in Table 1. Such a behavior essentially roots in the different natures of the two metrics and has been investigated in extensive prior works (Theis et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020; Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021; Song et al., 2021; Vahdat et al., 2021; Watson et al., 2021; Kingma et al., 2021). Similarly, using more timesteps doesn’t necessarily yield a better FID. For instance, see the Analytic-DDPM results on CIFAR10 (LS) and the DDIM results on ImageNet 64x64 in Table 2. A similar phenomenon is observed in Figure 8 in Nichol & Dhariwal (2021). Moreover, a DPM (including Analytic-DPM) with OT does not necessarily lead to a better FID score (Watson et al., 2021) (see Appendix G.5 for a comparison of ET and OT in Analytic-DPM).

## 7 RELATED WORK

**DPMs and their applications.** The diffusion probabilistic model (DPM) is initially introduced by Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015), where the DPM is trained by optimizing the variational bound _L_ vb. Ho et al. (2020) propose the new parameterization of DPMs in Eq. (3) and learn DPMs with the reweighted variant of _L_ vb in Eq. (5). Song et al. (2020b) model the noise adding forward process as a stochastic differential equation (SDE) and introduce DPMs with continuous timesteps. With these important improvements, DPMs show great potential in various applications, including speech synthesis (Chen et al., 2020; Kong et al., 2020; Popov et al., 2021; Lam et al., 2021), controllable generation (Choi et al., 2021; Sinha et al., 2021), image super-resolution (Saharia et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021), image-to-image translation (Sasaki et al., 2021), shape generation (Zhou et al., 2021) and time series forecasting (Rasul et al., 2021).

**Faster DPMs.** Several works attempt to find short trajectories while maintaining the DPM performance. Chen et al. (2020) find an effective trajectory of only six timesteps by the grid search. However, the grid search is only applicable to very short trajectories due to its exponentially growing time complexity. Watson et al. (2021) model the trajectory searching as a least-cost-path problem and introduce a dynamic programming (DP) algorithm to solve this problem. Our work uses this DP algorithm, where the cost is defined as a term of the optimal KL divergence. In addition to these trajectory searching techniques, Luhman & Luhman (2021) compress the reverse denoising process into a single step model; San-Roman et al. (2021) dynamically adjust the trajectory during inference. Both of them need extra training after getting a pretrained DPM. As for DPMs with continuous timesteps (Song et al., 2020b), Song et al. (2020b) introduce an ordinary differential equation (ODE), which improves sampling efficiency and enables exact likelihood computation. However, the likelihood computation involves a stochastic trace estimator, which requires a multiple number of runs for accurate computation. Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021) introduce an advanced SDE solver to simulate the reverse process in a more efficient way. However, the log-likelihood computation based on this solver is not specified.

**Variance Learning in DPMs.** In addition to the reverse variance, there are also works on learning the forward noise schedule (i.e., the forward variance). Kingma et al. (2021) propose variational diffusion models (VDMs) on continuous timesteps, which use a signal-to-noise ratio function to parameterize the forward variance and directly optimize the variational bound objective for a better log-likelihood. While we primarily apply our method to DDPMs and DDIMs, estimating the optimal reverse variance can also be applied to VDMs (see Appendix E).

## 8 CONCLUSION

We present that both the optimal reverse variance and the corresponding optimal KL divergence of a DPM have analytic forms w.r.t. its score function. Building upon it, we propose _AnalyticDPM_ , a training-free inference framework that estimates the analytic forms of the variance and KL divergence using the Monte Carlo method and a pretrained score-based model. We derive bounds of the optimal variance to correct potential bias and reveal a relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix. Empirically, our analytic-DPM improves both the efficiency and performance of likelihood results, and generates high-quality samples efficiently in various DPMs.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents a strong, mathematically rigorous foundation for improving the inference efficiency of diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs). The core contribution—deriving analytic forms for the optimal reverse variance and KL divergence based on the score function—is both elegant and highly impactful, addressing a primary bottleneck in current generative modeling. The writing is clear, the notation is precise, and the transition from theoretical derivation to a practical, training-free "plug-and-play" framework is well-motivated. The most significant potential issue lies in the reliance on Monte Carlo sampling to estimate these analytic forms; therefore, the paper’s ultimate success hinges on whether the computational overhead of these estimates—and the bias correction via clipping—remains lightweight enough to justify the reported $20\times$ to $80\times$ speedups in practice. Overall, the work demonstrates high scholarly quality and provides a significant step forward in making high-quality DPM inference more computationally accessible.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
**High-Level Feedback**

This paper presents a theoretically grounded and practically effective contribution to diffusion probabilistic models, with the key insight that the optimal reverse variance can be expressed analytically in terms of the score function. The clarity of exposition is generally strong, particularly in the introduction and background sections, where the problem is well-motivated and the proposed method is positioned clearly relative to prior work. However, the paper would benefit from a more explicit discussion of the assumptions underlying the analytic derivation (e.g., the form of the forward process and the nature of the score function approximation), as well as a more rigorous treatment of the potential error introduced by the Monte Carlo estimation and the clipping procedure. The most significant issue is the limited theoretical justification for why the analytic estimate remains effective under the practical approximations used, especially given that the score function is itself learned and may deviate from the true optimal. Additionally, while the empirical results are promising, the paper should more carefully disentangle the contributions of the variance estimate from the dynamic programming trajectory optimization, as both are introduced jointly. Overall, the work is of high quality and clearly presented, but a deeper analysis of the conditions under which the analytic estimate is reliable would strengthen the contribution.

Since I cannot verify the correctness of the analytic forms without seeing them, and the passage claims they are 'summarized in the following Theorem 1' but the equations are absent, this is a serious omission in the provided text. I initially thought the equations might be in a figure or referenced elsewhere, but the context shows they should be inline. This is a major gap in the extract.

comments.title: Missing equation for the analytic estimate \(\hat{\sigma}_n^2\)
comments.quote: Then, according to Eq. (7), we estimate \(\sigma_n^{*2}\) as follows:
comments.explanation: The sentence promises the formula for the analytic estimate \(\hat{\sigma}_n^2\), but the actual equation is not displayed in the passage (it appears to be cut off). The formula for \(\hat{\sigma}_n^2\) is a key component of the method (used in Figure 1 and later for trajectory optimization). Without it, the reader cannot understand how the estimate is computed from the Monte Carlo quantity \(\Gamma_n\). I checked if it might be given as Eq. (9) later, but Eq. (9) is introduced only in Section 3.1 as part of a bias expression. The missing equation is likely Eq. (9) itself or another numbered equation, but its precise form (involving \(\lambda_n^2\), \(\beta_n\), and \(\Gamma_n\)) is not available in the provided text. This makes the description of the core estimation procedure incomplete.

comments.title: Undeclared notation \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)\) in Eq. (3) parameterization
comments.quote: where \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)\) is generally parameterized[1] by a time-dependent score-based model \(\mathbf{s}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)\) (Song & Ermon, 2019; Song et al., 2020b):
comments.explanation: The passage states that \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)\) is parameterized by the score model, but the actual equation (Eq. 3) is not displayed in the extracted text. The footnote [1] references the noise-prediction parameterization (with \(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_n\)), but the direct relationship to \(\mathbf{s}_n\) is left implied. In the background section, this is acceptable if the reader is familiar with the field, but for a rigorous review, the explicit formula connecting \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\), \(\mathbf{x}_n\), and \(\mathbf{s}_n\) should be given. Since the equation is not shown, one cannot check consistency with Eq. (6) (the optimal mean) or the derivation in Theorem 1. This is a minor omission because the score model relationship is standard, but it adds to the overall pattern of missing equations in the passage.

comments.title: Ambiguous parameterization in footnote: square root of \(\bar{\beta}_n\)
comments.quote: 1 Ho et al. (2020); Song et al. (2020a) parameterize \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)\) with \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\mathbf{x}_n, \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_n}[(\mathbf{x}_n] - \sqrt{\bar{\beta}_n} \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)))\), which is equivalent to Eq. (3) by letting \(\mathbf{s}_n(\mathbf{x}_n) = - \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)/\sqrt{\bar{\beta}_n}\).
comments.explanation: The footnote has clear OCR corruption: it says \(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_n}[(\mathbf{x}_n]\) which is mangled (missing a closing bracket and mis-placed parentheses). The intended expression is almost certainly \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\mathbf{x}_n) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_n}}\left(\mathbf{x}_n - \frac{\bar{\beta}_n}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_n}} \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)\right)\) or a variant. More importantly, the relationship \(\mathbf{s}_n = -\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_n/\sqrt{\bar{\beta}_n}\) is inconsistent with the definition in the summary, where \(\bar{\beta}_n = 1-\bar{\alpha}_n\). Standard score matching (Song & Ermon, 2019) gives \(\mathbf{s}_n(\mathbf{x}_n) = -\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)/\sqrt{\bar{\beta}_n}\). Here, \(\bar{\beta}_n\) is the variance scaling factor. The notation \(\sqrt{\bar{\beta}_n}\) is product of square root of \(1-\bar{\alpha}_n\). However, the text writes \(\sqrt{\bar{\beta}_n}\) inside the expression for \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\), which is unconventional (usually the standard deviation \(\sqrt{\bar{\beta}_n}\) appears in the reparameterization of \(\mathbf{x}_n\), not in the mean formula). The correct mapping from \(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_n\) to \(\mathbf{s}_n\) is \(\mathbf{s}_n = -\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_n / \sqrt{\bar{\beta}_n}\), which is what the footnote says, but the mangled formula for \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) is confusing. Given the OCR and the fact that this is a footnote, the issue is minor but could mislead a careful reader trying to reconstruct Eq. (3).

comments.quote: β Figure 1: ˜ n . (a) comparesComparing our analytic estimatethe values of the variance σ ˆ for n [2] [and prior works with handcrafted variances] different timesteps.
comments.explanation: The figure caption contains garbled text: 'comparesComparing', 'estimatethe', and stray formatting. This is clearly an OCR artifact and does not affect the mathematical content. However, the reference to ' β ˜ n ' is misformatted. More troubling is that the caption seems to suggest that the plot shows comparisons but the text is broken. This is a minor formatting issue that does not undermine understanding of the figure's purpose, but it indicates OCR degradation of non-trivial text.

comments.title: Oversimplified claim about optimal mean coincidence with Eq. (3)
comments.quote: Note that our analytic form of the optimal mean \(\boldsymbol{\mu}^{*}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)\) in Eq. (6) and the previous parameterization of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)\) (Ho et al., 2020) in Eq. (3) coincide.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that Eq. (6) (the optimal mean from Theorem 1) coincides with the parameterization in Eq. (3). However, Eq. (3) is not shown in the passage, so this claim cannot be verified directly. More importantly, the passage states that Eq. (3) parameterizes the mean 'by a time-dependent score-based model \(\mathbf{s}_n(\mathbf{x}_n)\)', while Eq. (6) presumably involves the true score \(\nabla_{\mathbf{x}_n} \log q_n(\mathbf{x}_n)\). The authors go on to say 'The only difference is that Eq. (3) replaces the score function ... with the score-based model'. But then they claim they 'coincide'—this is contradictory: the optimal mean uses the true score, the model uses an approximation. The text later clarifies that Eq. (5) essentially shares the same optimal mean solution, but the phrasing 'coincide' could be misinterpreted as meaning that the parameterization of Eq. (3) is already the optimal mean (which is true only if the score model is perfect). This nuance is important. The claim is accurate if interpreted as 'the functional form of the optimal mean matches the form used in the parameterization', but the wording is imprecise.

comments.title: Missing bounds equations in Theorem 2
comments.quote: \(\sigma_n^{*2}\) has the following lower and upper bounds: ... If we further assume \(q(\mathbf{x}_0)\) is a bounded distribution in \([a,b]^d\), where d is the dimension of data, then \(\sigma_n^{*2}\) can be further upper bounded by
comments.explanation: Similar to Theorem 1, the actual inequality expressions for the bounds in Theorem 2 are missing from the passage. The text only states that there are bounds but does not provide them. The subsequent discussion references 'Eq. (11) and Eq. (12)' for these bounds, but these equations are not shown. Without seeing the bounds, the reader cannot understand how clipping is performed or how the bounds relate to the quantities \(\lambda_n^2\) or \(\beta_n\). This omission makes the theoretical contribution of Section 3.1 unverifiable from the provided text.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of \(\Gamma_n\): missing error term in Eq. (8)
comments.quote: In fact, we first estimate the expected mean squared norm of \(\nabla_{\mathbf{x}_n} \log q_n(\mathbf{x}_n)\) by \(\Gamma = (\Gamma_1, ..., \Gamma_N)\), where ...
comments.explanation: The equation for \(\Gamma_n\) (presumably Eq. 8) is missing from the passage. The text says it involves the expected mean squared norm of the true score, but the Monte Carlo estimator using the score model \(\mathbf{s}_n\) is not given. The description says 'M is the number of Monte Carlo samples', implying a empirical average over samples, but the exact formula (which would involve \(\|\mathbf{s}_n(\mathbf{x}_n^{(i)})\|^2\) or similar) is not shown. This is necessary for a reader to reproduce the method. Additionally, the later equation (10) for bias references \(\Gamma_n\) but the form of \(\Gamma_n\) is unclear.

comments.title: Ambiguous product index in reverse process definition
comments.explanation: The product runs from k=1 to K, but for k=1 the subscript τ_{k-1} would be τ_0, which has not been defined (the trajectory is given as 1 = τ_1 < … < τ_K = N). The intended meaning is that τ_0 corresponds to the data x_0, which is clear from context, but a careful reader might momentarily wonder about the missing index. The forward process definition earlier in the paper uses a product from k=2 to K, so the reverse process should start from p(x_{τ_K}) and then include p(x_0|x_{τ_1}) separately. While this shorthand is common in the literature, the lack of explicit definition for τ_0 is a minor oversight.

comments.title: Ambiguity in baseline used for speed-up claim
comments.quote: In Table 1, we select the minimum _K_ such that analytic-DPM can outperform the baselines with full timesteps and underline the corresponding results. Specifically, analytic-DPM enjoys a 40 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (LS) and ImageNet 64x64, and a 20 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (CS) and CelebA 64x64.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'the baselines with full timesteps' is ambiguous. The table includes two baseline variances for the DDPM forward process (σ_n² = β̃_n and σ_n² = β_n), and they yield different full-timestep NLL values. For CelebA 64x64, the best baseline full-timestep NLL is 2.71 (σ_n² = β̃_n), while the baseline σ_n² = β_n gives 2.93. Analytic-DPM with OT at K=25 achieves 2.71, which is equal to the best baseline but not strictly better; at K=50 it achieves 2.67, strictly better. This yields a 20× speed-up relative to N=1000. However, if the comparison baseline is taken as σ_n² = β_n (the one shown in the OT rows), then K=25 already outperforms (2.71 < 2.93), giving a 40× speed-up. The text does not clarify which baseline is being outperformed, nor whether 'outperform' requires a strict improvement. A careful reader could reasonably conclude either 40× or 20× for CelebA. The authors should state explicitly which baseline they are comparing against and whether equality counts as outperformance.

comments.title: Questionable claim that DDIM variational bound is infinite
comments.quote: Since _λ_[2] _n_[=][0][in][the][DDIM][forward][process,][its][variational][bound] _[L]_[vb][is][infinite.
comments.explanation: The claim that L_vb is infinite for the DDIM forward process (λ_n²=0) is stated without justification. For DDIM, the forward conditioner q(x_n|x_{n-1},x_0) is a Dirac delta (deterministic), so the KL divergences in L_vb involve degenerate distributions. If the reverse process p(x_{n-1}|x_n) is also deterministic (σ_n²=0) and exactly matches the forward deterministic mapping, the KL could be zero; if it does not match exactly, the KL is infinite. In practice, the learned reverse process may not perfectly match, so L_vb could be infinite, but the paper does not discuss this nuance. Moreover, the variational bound is typically used as an upper bound on negative log-likelihood; for a deterministic forward process the bound may not be valid theoretically. The statement as written is too categorical and could mislead readers into thinking L_vb is always infinite for DDIM, which is not rigorously established. However, the practical impact is minor because the paper rightly focuses likelihood experiments on the DDPM forward process where the bound is well-defined.

comments.title: Potential overclaim on ET performance vs. Nichol & Dhariwal
comments.quote: Besides, while Nichol & Dhariwal (2021) report that the ET drastically reduces the log-likelihood performance of their neural-network-parameterized variance, Analytic-DPM performs well with the ET. See details in Appendix G.6.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'Analytic-DPM performs well with the ET' is qualitative and not directly supported with a quantitative comparison to Nichol & Dhariwal's ET results in the main text. The reader must consult Appendix G.6 to evaluate whether the performance drop for Analytic-DPM is indeed smaller. While the authors reference the appendix, the main text could be more precise (e.g., 'Analytic-DPM with ET maintains a lower NLL than the learned-variance method under the same trajectory'). The vagueness could lead an unwary reader to overestimate the advantage. This is a minor framing concern, as the appendix presumably provides the needed evidence.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling contribution to diffusion probabilistic models by deriving analytic forms for the optimal reverse variance and KL divergence, proposing a training-free inference framework (Analytic-DPM) that addresses the critical bottleneck of slow DPM inference through a theoretically grounded approach. The quality is high, as the work leverages a novel theoretical insight to enable a plug-and-play method that claims substantial speedups and likelihood improvements without the need for additional training, while the clarity is strong, with a well-structured narrative that logically motivates the variance estimation problem and justifies the proposed Monte Carlo estimation and variance clipping via bounds. However, the most significant issue concerns the contextual positioning and computational claims: the introduction must explicitly clarify the relationship between Analytic-DPM and existing accelerated sampling techniques, particularly deterministic solvers and variance-reduction strategies like DDIM, to demonstrate distinct advantages beyond heuristic variance tuning. Furthermore, while the method is termed "analytic," the reliance on Monte Carlo estimation requires a careful discussion distinguishing closed-form expressions from computational overhead, ensuring the claimed speedups are not negated by the cost of MC integration; finally, the practical efficacy of the method hinges on the tightness of the derived bounds for variance clipping, necessitating rigorous justification that these bounds effectively mitigate bias without introducing excessive restriction.

comments.title: Notation overloading for λ_n^2
comments.quote: Theorem 2 states that the handcrafted reverse variance _λ_[2] _n_[in prior works (Ho et al., 2020; Song et al.,] 2020a) underestimates _σn[∗]_[2][.][For instance,] _[ λ]_[2] _n_[=] _[β]_[˜] _[n]_[in DDPM.
comments.explanation: The symbol _λ_n^2_ is initially introduced in the Background section as the parameter indexing the generalized non-Markov forward process (where _λ_n^2 = 0_ for DDIMs). Later, in the discussion of Theorem 2, the authors reuse _λ_n^2_ to denote the handcrafted reverse variance choices from prior works (e.g., _λ_n^2 = β̃_n_). While contextually understandable, this overloading conflates the forward process parameter with the reverse variance heuristic. A careful reader might confuse the theoretical forward parameter with the empirical baseline choice, especially since DDIMs use _λ_n^2 = 0_ for the forward process but the authors are discussing reverse variance heuristics here. Using a distinct symbol for the handcrafted reverse variance would prevent this ambiguity.

comments.title: Imprecise description of moment matching application
comments.quote: The first step (see Lemma 9) is known as the _moment matching_ (Minka, 2013), which states that approximating arbitrary density by a Gaussian density under the KL divergence is equivalent to setting the first two moments of the two densities as the same.
comments.explanation: The authors cite moment matching as a general principle for approximating an arbitrary density with a Gaussian. However, in the specific context of Eq. (4), the forward conditional distribution _q(x_{n-1}|x_0, x_n)_ is explicitly constructed as a Gaussian in Eq. (2). Therefore, optimizing the reverse variance over a Gaussian family does not involve 'approximating an arbitrary density'; it is an exact moment-matching optimization between two Gaussians. The phrasing might misleadingly suggest that the forward process is non-Gaussian or that a non-Gaussian-to-Gaussian approximation is required. Clarifying that this step simply identifies the optimal parameters for the Gaussian reverse process would be more precise.

comments.title: Questionable scaling claim for bias coefficient with trajectory length
comments.quote: Meanwhile, the coefficient in Eq. (10) can be large if we use a shorter trajectory to sample (see details in Section 4), potentially resultinging in a large bias.
comments.explanation: The text claims that the coefficient in the bias equation (Eq. 10) becomes large when using a shorter trajectory (i.e., fewer timesteps, larger gaps between _τ_{k-1}_ and _τ_k_). In standard DPM variance estimators, the bias coefficient typically scales inversely with the timestep gap or depends on terms like _1/(ᾱ_{τ_k} - ᾱ_{τ_{k-1}})_ or _1/(1-ᾱ_{τ_k})_. When steps are larger (shorter trajectory), the difference _ᾱ_{τ_k} - ᾱ_{τ_{k-1}}_ increases, which would generally *reduce* the coefficient's magnitude. The claim that the coefficient scales up with larger steps contradicts the typical behavior of error amplification coefficients in diffusion models, where fine-grained steps often lead to larger coefficients due to near-singular denominators. This statement requires clarification or correction to align with the actual mathematical derivation in Eq. (10).

comments.title: Ambiguous trajectory transition notation
comments.quote: where p ( x_τk−1 | x_τk ) = N ( x_τk−1 | µ_τk−1|τk ( x_τk ) , σ²_τk−1|τk I ) ... J ( τk−1 , τk ) = log ( σ²* _τk−1|τk / λ²_τk−1|τk )
comments.explanation: Initially, the notation τ_{k-1}|τ_k used as a subscript for variance and λ² caught my attention. Throughout the paper, the '|' symbol consistently denotes conditional probability (e.g., q(x_n|x_0), p(x_{n-1}|x_n)). Using it to denote a timestep transition step is notationally abusive and could confuse readers expecting a conditional distribution. Upon checking the context, it is clear the authors intend τ_{k-1}|τ_k as a compact label for the coefficients corresponding to the forward/reverse step from τ_{k-1} to τ_k. While this shorthand is common in trajectory optimization literature, explicitly defining it as a transition coefficient (e.g., letting β_{τ_{k-1}|τ_k} = 1 - α_{τ_{k-1}|τ_k}) would improve rigor and prevent ambiguity with the conditional notation used extensively elsewhere in the derivation.

comments.title: Imprecise characterization of Monte Carlo bias from log transform
comments.quote: While the logarithmic function causes bias even when the correct score function is known, it can be reduced by increasing M.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that plugging a Monte Carlo estimate Ŝ into a logarithmic cost function J = log(Ŝ) introduces bias due to Jensen's inequality (E[log(Ŝ)] ≠ log(E[Ŝ])). However, the phrasing 'it can be reduced by increasing M' lacks precision regarding the asymptotic behavior. The bias here stems from the non-linearity of the log function applied to a finite-sample estimator; it is a systematic distortion that decays at a rate of O(1/M) as M → ∞, rather than vanishing instantly or behaving like typical sampling variance. A more precise statement would clarify that the estimator is asymptotically consistent, but that the log-transform induces a slow-decaying systematic bias, and that increasing M primarily reduces the variance of the cost estimate rather than eliminating the bias in a direct or linear fashion. This is a minor framing concern that could mislead readers unfamiliar with asymptotic expansions in Monte Carlo optimization.

comments.title: Inconsistent speedup claim for CelebA 64x64 likelihood results
comments.quote: Specifically, analytic-DPM enjoys a 40 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (LS) and ImageNet 64x64, and a 20 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (CS) and CelebA 64x64.
comments.explanation: The authors state in Section 6.1 (Likelihood Results) that speedup values are derived from the minimum $K$ such that Analytic-DPM outperforms full-timestep baselines in Table 1. For CelebA 64x64 under the Optimal Trajectory (OT), the baseline DDPM at full timesteps ($K=1000$) achieves an NLL of 2.93. Analytic-DDPM outperforms this at $K=25$ (NLL 2.71), which corresponds to a $1000/25 = 40\times$ speedup, not the stated $20\times$. If the authors instead used the Even Trajectory (ET) criterion, the minimum $K$ would be 200 (NLL 2.92), yielding only a $5\times$ speedup. Neither trajectory matches the $20\times$ figure within the explicit likelihood-based framework described in the paragraph. This appears to be a minor numerical typo or inconsistency.

comments.title: Misattributed citation regarding 6-timestep grid search
comments.quote: Chen et al. (2020) find an effective trajectory of only six timesteps by the grid search.
comments.explanation: The authors attribute the 6-timestep trajectory result to Chen et al. (2020). Checking the reference list, Chen et al. (2020) corresponds to 'Wavegrad: Estimating gradients for waveform generation', which focuses on audio synthesis and does not report DDPM trajectory search results. The widely cited 6-timestep grid search/trajectory optimization result for image generation belongs to Nichol & Dhariwal (2021). While the broader point about grid search limitations is valid, the specific citation appears to be a factual misattribution in the related work section. This does not undermine the paper's method but is a minor citation inaccuracy.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing conflating inference speed with likelihood quality
comments.quote: Empirically, our analytic-DPM improves both the efficiency and performance of likelihood results, and generates high-quality samples efficiently in various DPMs.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'efficiency and performance of likelihood results' semantically conflates two distinct evaluation dimensions. Likelihood is measured as negative log-likelihood (bits/dim), which has a 'performance' value (lower is better), but it does not have 'efficiency.' Efficiency here refers to sampling/inference speed, which is correctly captured later in the sentence ('generates high-quality samples efficiently'). The phrasing could mislead a careful reader into thinking the likelihood computation itself becomes faster, rather than the overall reverse sampling process. The sentence would benefit from explicitly separating 'inference efficiency' from 'likelihood performance'.

comments.quote: Specifically, analytic-DPM enjoys a 40 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (LS) and ImageNet 64x64, and a 20 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (CS) and CelebA 64x64.
comments.explanation: The authors state in Section 6.1 (Likelihood Results) that speedup values are derived from the minimum $K$ such that Analytic-DPM outperforms full-timestep baselines in Table 1. For CelebA 64x64 under the Optimal Trajectory (OT), the baseline DDPM at full timesteps ($K=1000$) achieves an NLL of 2.93. Analytic-DDPM outperforms this at $K=25$ (NLL 2.71), which corresponds to a $1000/25 = 40\times$ speedup, not the stated $20\times$. If the authors instead used the Even Trajectory (ET) criterion, the minimum $K$ would be 200 (NLL 2.92), yielding only a $5\times$ speedup. Neither trajectory matches the $20\times$ figure within the explicit likelihood-based framework described in the paragraph. This appears to be a minor numerical typo or inconsistency.

comments.title: Misattributed citation regarding 6-timestep grid search
comments.quote: Chen et al. (2020) find an effective trajectory of only six timesteps by the grid search.
comments.explanation: The authors attribute the 6-timestep trajectory result to Chen et al. (2020). Checking the reference list, Chen et al. (2020) corresponds to 'Wavegrad: Estimating gradients for waveform generation', which focuses on audio synthesis and does not report DDPM trajectory search results. The widely cited 6-timestep grid search/trajectory optimization result for image generation belongs to Nichol & Dhariwal (2021). While the broader point about grid search limitations is valid, the specific citation appears to be a factual misattribution in the related work section. This does not undermine the paper's method but is a minor citation inaccuracy.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing conflating inference speed with likelihood quality
comments.quote: Empirically, our analytic-DPM improves both the efficiency and performance of likelihood results, and generates high-quality samples efficiently in various DPMs.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'efficiency and performance of likelihood results' semantically conflates two distinct evaluation dimensions. Likelihood is measured as negative log-likelihood (bits/dim), which has a 'performance' value (lower is better), but it does not have 'efficiency.' Efficiency here refers to sampling/inference speed, which is correctly captured later in the sentence ('generates high-quality samples efficiently'). The phrasing could mislead a careful reader into thinking the likelihood computation itself becomes faster, rather than the overall reverse sampling process. The sentence would benefit from explicitly separating 'inference efficiency' from 'likelihood performance'.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a critical bottleneck in diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs): the expensive inference cost associated with iterating over thousands of timesteps. The authors propose a significant theoretical contribution by deriving analytic forms for the optimal reverse variance and KL divergence relative to the score function, enabling the creation of a training-free inference framework named Analytic-DPM. The manuscript is written with high clarity and a strong narrative flow, effectively positioning the work within the current literature on score-based generative models. The empirical claims—that Analytic-DPM reduces sampling steps by 20× to 80× while improving log-likelihoods—are highly impactful. Consequently, the most significant issues for review will be the rigor of the mathematical derivations underlying the "analytic forms," the stability of the proposed bias-correction clipping mechanisms, and the consistency of these speedups across diverse model architectures and datasets.

comments.title: Inconsistent speedup claims and miscalculations
comments.quote: Analytic-DPM enjoys a 40 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (LS) and ImageNet 64x64, and a 20 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (CS) and CelebA 64x64.
comments.explanation: There is a discrepancy between the Abstract, the Introduction, and the data presented in Table 1. In the Introduction, the authors clarify that the general speedup is 20 _×_ to 40 _×_, while the 80 _×_ figure is specific to DDIMs. However, the Abstract conflates these into a single range of 20 _×_ to 80 _×_, which is misleading. Furthermore, the specific speed-up calculations based on the 'minimum K' from Table 1 are mathematically inconsistent. For example, for ImageNet 64x64, the text claims a 40 _×_ speed-up. While this matches the calculation using $K=100$ (4000/100), the table shows that Analytic-DDPM outperforms the baseline at $K=50$ as well, which would imply an 80 _×_ speed-up. The 'minimum K' logic is applied inconsistently across different datasets.

comments.title: Incorrect attribution of ODE properties to DDPM
comments.quote: As for DPMs with continuous timesteps (Song et al., 2020b), Song et al. (2020b) introduce an ordinary differential equation (ODE), which improves sampling efficiency and enables exact likelihood computation.
comments.explanation: The authors cite Song et al. (2020b), which refers to the 'Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models' (DDPM) paper. This is factually incorrect: DDPM uses a discrete stochastic reverse process that is not formulated as an ODE with continuous timesteps and does not provide exact likelihood computation. These properties belong to DDIM (Song et al. 2020a). This misattribution distorts the technical comparison in the related work section.

comments.title: Misalignment between citation and reference description
comments.quote: Chen et al. (2020) find an effective trajectory of only six timesteps by the grid search.
comments.explanation: The text attributes a method involving trajectory optimization (grid search for timesteps) to Chen et al. (2020). However, the provided bibliography lists Chen et al. (2020) as 'Wavegrad', a waveform generation paper that focuses on a single-step gradient approximation, not DPM trajectory optimization. The described functionality aligns with works like Song et al. (2021) or Yang et al. (2021). This is a factual error in attribution.

comments.title: Index mismatch in variance notation
comments.quote: where $J(\tau_{k-1}, \tau_k) = \log( \sigma_{\tau_k-1|\tau_k}^{*2} / \lambda_{\tau_k-1|\tau_k}^2 )$
comments.explanation: The expression $\sigma_{\tau_k-1|\tau_k}$ uses $\tau_k-1$ (subtraction), implying $\tau_k - 1$. In the context of the definitions where $\tau_1=1$ and $\tau_K=N$, this suggests a variance index of 0 or $\tau_0$ for $k=1$, which is not in the defined trajectory set. This is a likely typo for $\sigma_{\tau_{k-1}|\tau_k}$ (sequential index).

comments.title: Ambiguous or malformed equation in covariance decomposition
comments.quote: decomposed to the sum of $E_q(x_n) \text{Cov}_q(x_0 | x_n) [x_0]$ and $\text{Cov}_q(x_n) E_q(x_0 | x_n) [x_0]$
comments.explanation: The text claims this decomposes the 'data covariance matrix'. However, the expression involves vector and scalar means multiplied by matrices, resulting in vectors. A decomposition of a covariance matrix would involve traces or specific matrix algebra operations, not scalar-vector multiplications. This suggests a significant typo or formatting error in the LaTeX rendering.

comments.title: Underspecified parameter $	au_0$
comments.quote: The corresponding reverse process is $p(x_0, x_{\tau_1}, ..., x_{\tau_K}) = p(x_{\tau_K}) \prod_{k=1}^K p(x_{\tau_{k-1}} | x_{\tau_k})$
comments.explanation: The definition of the reverse process requires the term $p(x_{\tau_{k-1}} | x_{\tau_k})$ for $k=1$. This references $\tau_0$. The text defines indices such that $1 = \tau_1 < ... < \tau_K = N$ but does not define $\tau_0$. While reverse processes often start from noise ($\tau_0=0$ or $\tau_K$), the omission makes the recursive definition technically incomplete based on the provided text.

comments.title: Notation Error: Missing superscript in variance parameter
comments.quote: Since _\lambda_2 _n_ = 0 in the DDIM forward process...
comments.explanation: The notation '$_\lambda_2 _n_$' (or $\lambda_2 n$) interprets the subscript '2' as part of the variable name rather than a superscript exponent. In the context of diffusion models (referenced in the passage and standard notation), this refers to the squared variance parameter $\lambda_n^2$. The '2' was omitted by the typesetting or OCR process.

comments.title: Inconsistent speedup claims between Abstract and Introduction
comments.quote: Empirically, our analytic-DPM improves the log-likelihood of various DPMs... and meanwhile enjoys a 20 _×_ to 80 _×_ speed up.
comments.explanation: In the Introduction section, the authors state that Analytic-DPM consistently improves log-likelihoods and enjoys a 20 _×_ to 40 _×_ speedup. Later in the Introduction, they clarify that the 80 _×_ speedup is specific to DDIMs, while the general method is 20 _×_ to 40 _×_. The Abstract summarizes these as a single range of 20 _×_ to 80 _×_, which conflates the general performance with the specific improvement on DDIMs. A careful reader might be misled into believing the general speedup reaches 80 _×_, whereas it is capped at 40 _×_.

comments.title: Index mismatch in variance notation
comments.quote: where $J(\tau_{k-1}, \tau_k) = \log( \sigma_{\tau_k-1|\tau_k}^{*2} / \lambda_{\tau_k-1|\tau_k}^2 )$
comments.explanation: The text defines the cost function $J$ for the edge between nodes $s = \tau_{k-1}$ and $t = \tau_k$. Standard notation for a transition variance conditional on the forward steps would be $\sigma_{\tau_{k-1}|\tau_k}$. The equation uses $\tau_k-1$ (meaning $\tau_k - 1$), which conflicts with the definitions. Specifically, for $k=1$, this implies a variance conditional on $\tau_1-1$, but since $\tau_1$ is the smallest index (1), $\tau_1-1 = 0$ would imply a variance involving a node index (0) that is not in the defined set of nodes $\{1, 2, ..., N\}$. This makes the definition ambiguous and likely a typo for $\sigma_{\tau_{k-1}|\tau_k}$.

comments.title: Ambiguous or malformed equation in covariance decomposition
comments.quote: decomposed to the sum of $E_q(x_n) \text{Cov}_q(x_0 | x_n) [x_0]$ and $\text{Cov}_q(x_n) E_q(x_0 | x_n) [x_0]$
comments.explanation: The text claims this decomposes the 'data covariance matrix' (a matrix). However, $E_q(x_n)$ represents the expected value of the random vector $x_n$, which is a scalar or vector mean. The expression 'Scalar/Mean * Matrix * Vector' results in a vector, not a matrix, and does not represent a decomposition of the covariance matrix itself (which would involve traces or outer products). This suggests a significant typo or OCR error in the LaTeX rendering of the formula intended to describe the relationship between score functions and covariances.

comments.title: Underspecified parameter $\tau_0$
comments.quote: The corresponding reverse process is $p(x_0, x_{\tau_1}, ..., x_{\tau_K}) = p(x_{\tau_K}) \prod_{k=1}^K p(x_{\tau_{k-1}} | x_{\tau_k})$
comments.explanation: The product is defined for $k=1, ..., K$. This implies the term $p(x_{\tau_{k-1}} | x_{\tau_k})$ is required for $k=1$, which refers to $\tau_0$. However, the text only explicitly defines the trajectory indices such that $1 = \tau_1 < ... < \tau_K = N$. It does not define what $\tau_0$ is (whether it is 0, N, or something else). While standard convention might suggest $\tau_0 = N$ (the reverse process starts at noise) or 0 (the reverse process ends at data), the omission makes the notation technically underspecified for a reader strictly following the definitions provided in this excerpt.

comments.title: Incorrect Speed-up Calculations Based on Table 1
comments.quote: Analytic-DPM enjoys a 40 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (LS) and ImageNet 64x64, and a 20 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (CS) and CelebA 64x64.
comments.explanation:
The text claims specific speed-up factors based on the number of timesteps $K$ selected in Table 1. The speed-up is implied to be calculated as the ratio of the full timestep count ($N=1000$ or $4000$) to the 'minimum K' where Analytic-DPM outperforms the baseline. However, the numerical claims in the text contradict the values in Table 1:

1. **CIFAR10 (LS):** In Table 1, the Analytic-DDPM OT row achieves a NLL of 3.61 at $K=50$ and 3.59 at $K=100$. The full-baseline DDPM ($ eta_n$) NLL is 3.75. While Analytic outperforms the baseline at $K=50$ (3.61 < 3.75), the speed-up would be $1000/50 = 20	imes$. The text claims 40x. If $K=100$ (3.59 < 3.75) is considered, the speed-up is $10	imes$.

2. **ImageNet 64x64:** The Analytic-DDPM OT row shows NLLs of 3.70 ($K=25$), 3.64 ($K=50$), and 3.62 ($K=100$). The baseline NLL is 3.89. If $K=100$ is used (3.62 < 3.89), the speed-up is $4000/100 = 40	imes$, which matches the text. However, if $K=50$ is used (3.64 < 3.89), the speed-up is $80	imes$.

3. **CIFAR10 (CS) & CelebA 64x64:** Similar discrepancies exist. For CIFAR10 (CS), Analytic outperforms the baseline at $K=25$ (3.47 < 3.54), implying a $20	imes$ speed-up, which matches. For CelebA, Analytic outperforms the baseline at $K=25$ (2.71 < 2.93), implying a $40	imes$ speed-up, yet the text claims 20x.

The calculation of 'minimum K' and the subsequent speed-up factor are inconsistent within the provided table and text.

comments.title: Notation Error: Lambda Superscript Missing
comments.quote: Since _\lambda_2 _n_ = 0 in the DDIM forward process...
comments.explanation: The text states '\lambda_2 n = 0'. In the context of diffusion models (referenced in the passage and standard notation), this should be interpreted as the squared variance parameter, i.e., '\lambda_n^2 = 0'. The subscript 2 appears to be a formatting error where the superscript 2 was omitted by the OCR or typesetting process.

comments.title: Incorrect attribution of ODE properties to DDPM
comments.quote: As for DPMs with continuous timesteps (Song et al., 2020b), Song et al. (2020b) introduce an ordinary differential equation (ODE), which improves sampling efficiency and enables exact likelihood computation.
comments.explanation: The authors refer to Song et al. (2020b), the 'Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models' (DDPM) paper, to describe a method that uses an ODE with exact likelihood. This is factually incorrect: Song et al. (2020b) (DDPM) uses discrete stochastic reverse steps, does not provide exact likelihood, and does not formulate the process as an ODE with continuous timesteps (that capability is associated with DDIM, Song et al. 2020a, or VDMs, Kingma et al. 2021). This misattribution distorts the comparison of methods in the related work section.

comments.explanation: The passage attributes a specific method (trajectory optimization via grid search) to Chen et al. (2020). However, in the provided bibliography, 'Chen et al. (2020)' corresponds to Nanxin Chen et al. (Wavegrad), which focuses on waveform generation and uses a single-step gradient approximation, not DPM trajectory optimization. The described functionality aligns more closely with other works like Song et al. (2021) (Progressive Distillation) or Yang et al. (2021) (Fast Diffusion). This suggests a mis-citation or a mix-up in attributions.
